title: Enhanced Density Fluctuations Near a Disordered Chiral Topological Transition
authors: Hai-Tao Ding, Sen Mu, Leong-Chuan Kwek, Gabriel Lemarié, Jiangbin Gong
abstract: The universal statistics of density fluctuations of localized quantum states may offer unprecedented opportunities to probe and understand quantum transport in connection with dimensionality, coherence, symmetry and disorder. To date, the possible role of topological phase transitions in the fluctuation statistics is not studied yet. Using a Su-Schrieffer-Heeger chain subject to off-diagonal disorder (so that chiral symmetry is preserved), this work investigates how a disorder driven topological phase transition impacts on the spatial fluctuations of the logarithmic wave-packet density $\ln P(r)$ at distance $r$ from the initial excitation. Away from the transition, in both topological and trivial localized phases, the standard deviation follows the conventional one-dimensional scaling $σ[\ln P(r)]\sim r^θ$ with $θ\simeq 1/2$. Near the transition, however, the fluctuation growth is enhanced: the fitted exponent $θ$ increases above $1/2$ in a nonmonotonic manner before returning close to $1/2$ at criticality. We interpret this behavior from the energy-resolved density of states and localization length. Near the transition, several energy sectors carry appreciable spectral weight and exhibit competitive decay rates, preventing a single localization scale from dominating the accessible wave-packet tail and thereby enhancing the fluctuations of $\ln P(r)$. Our results establish wave-packet fluctuation statistics as a dynamical diagnostic of disordered chiral topological transitions and motivate broader studies of fluctuation phenomena in disordered topological quantum systems.
summary: This work examines how disorder-induced topological phase transitions affect quantum transport by analyzing the fluctuations in the wave-packet density. The key finding is that these fluctuations are anomalously enhanced near the transition point due to multiple energy scales contributing, establishing wave-packet statistics as a powerful diagnostic tool for such transitions.
main_problem: The study investigates how a disorder-driven topological phase transition affects the spatial fluctuations of the logarithmic wave-packet density, $\ln P(r)$, in localized quantum states.
main_result: The fluctuation growth exponent ($	heta$) is enhanced above the conventional 1D value ($	heta \simeq 1/2$) near the transition due to multiple energy sectors contributing, but returns to $\simeq 1/2$ at the critical point.
method: The analysis uses wave-packet propagation simulations and theoretical characterization via the zero-energy transfer-matrix recursion to diagnose the transition.
model_or_system: The model is a Su-Schrieffer-Heeger (SSH) chain subjected to off-diagonal disorder, which preserves chiral symmetry. The system is analyzed across topological (TI/TAI) and trivial insulating phases.
key_observables: Standard deviation of the logarithmic wave-packet density, $\sigma[\ln P(r)]$, and the associated scaling exponent $	heta$.
important_parameters: Disorder strength ($W$), hopping ratios ($t_1, t_2$), and the energy $E$ relative to zero energy.
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on the thermodynamic limit ($N 	o \infty$) and the applicability of power-law fitting for scaling exponents.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the phase diagram (TI, TAI, NI) based on winding numbers, the energy-resolved density of states, and plots showing the fluctuation exponent $	heta$ vs. disorder strength, highlighting non-monotonic behavior.
paper_structure: The paper first establishes the physical model and diagnostic tools (e.g., $\sigma[\ln P(r)]$). It then analyzes the system away from the transition, showing conventional scaling. The core of the work focuses on the non-monotonic enhancement of $	heta$ near the transition, followed by a discussion of the underlying multi-scale spectral weight contributions.
why_interesting: The finding that wave-packet fluctuation statistics serve as a dynamical diagnostic for topological transitions is highly relevant for understanding transport in disordered quantum materials, connecting localization theory to topological invariants.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) The phase diagram in the (t1, W) plane. Both TI and TAI regions are characterized by winding number v = 1, while the NI region has v = 0. The TAI phase is distinguished from the TI phase in that it is disorder-induced from a regime that is trivial in the clean limit. Analytically derived phase boundary from zero-energy transfer-matrix recursion is shown with the red dashed line, see [49, 61] and Appendix A for more details. (b1-b2) Disorder-averaged energy gap log10⟨∆E⟩as a function of t1, with W = 1.43. (b1) t1 ∈(0.1, 2), the gap closing at the transition point tc 1 = 1.40; (b2) Scaling of the disorder averaged energy gap ⟨∆E⟩versus |t1 −tc 1| on a double-logarithmic scale. The...

FIG. 2. Disorder-averaged logarithm of the wave density ⟨ln P(r)⟩at three evolution times, T1 = 103, T2 = 104, T3 = 105, for four representative values of t1. (a) t1 = 0.29 (TI), the profiles at different times nearly collapse and are well fitted by an exponential decay, with the localization length ξ = 3.2. (b) t1 = 1.12 (TAI), the profiles also approach a common exponential form at long times, with the localiza- tion length ξ = 3.3. (c) The disorder-averaged logarithm of the wave density for t1 = tc 1 = 1.40 at the critical point (CP). the profile exhibits pronounced time dependence and deviates markedly from a simple exponential form, due to the subexpo- nential decay of the typical...

FIG. 4. Disorder-averaged density of states ⟨ρ(E)⟩across the chiral topological transition. (a) ⟨ρ(E)⟩as a function of the mean intracell hopping strength t1 and energy E. The four vertical dashed lines mark four representative values of t1 used in panels (b)–(e). (b)–(e) Energy dependence of ⟨ρ(E)⟩for t1 = 0.29 (TI), t1 = 1.12 (TAI), t1 = 1.40 (CP), and t1 = 1.90 (NI), respectively. At t1 = 1.12, the system is still gapped, and the central peak corresponds to near-zero-energy states rather than states exactly at E = 0. FIG. 3. The fluctuation of the logarithm of the wave density ln P(r) with distance r. The blue circles represent the numer- ical data, the solid red line shows a power-law fit, the black dashed line indicates the scaling y = r1/2 for reference. (a) t1 = 0.29 (TI), the growth exponent is 0.53. (b) t1 = 1.12 (TAI), the growth exponent is 0.65. (c) t1 = 1.40 (CP), the growth exponent is 0.54. (d) t1 = 1.90 (NI), the growth ex- ponent is 0.52. (e) Fitted growth exponent θ as function of the mean intracell hopping strength t1. The four black open circles in panel (e) mark the four representative values of t1 used in panels (a)–(d). The two dashed vertical lines separate the TI, TAI, and NI...

FIG. 5. The energy-resolved localization length ξ(E) for four representative values of t1. (a) t1 = 0.29 (TI). (b) t1 = 1.12 (TAI). (c) t1 = 1.40 (CP). (d) t1 = 1.90 (NI). The localization length is obtained from the inverse magnitude of the disorder- averaged Lyapunov exponent. In the TI and NI regimes, ξ(E) remains finite over the relevant energy range, indicating that the long-distance wave-packet tail is governed by a stable effective decay scale. In the near-critical TAI regime, sev- eral energy sectors have comparable but distinct localization lengths, so the tail is not selected by a single decay chan- nel; this competition accounts for the enhanced fluctuation of ln P(r).
